deep_dive.title: Multi-agent Framework for Time-Sensitive Complementary Collaboration in Minecraft
deep_dive.abstract: We present TickingCollabBench, a Minecraft-based multi-agent benchmark for a novel class of time-sensitive complementary collaboration tasks. Our benchmark reflects four core characteristics of real-world collaboration: agent heterogeneity, mandatory collaboration, dynamic environments, and strict real-time constraints with failure risks. To enable this, we develop the TickingCollab framework, which supports the generation of diverse dynamic environments and abstracts Minecraft's primitive APIs to enable declarative YAML task specifications for composing these events. Building on this, we design a feasibility-aware automated benchmark generation pipeline, where an LLM drafts structurally diverse task configurations and feasibility verifier filters out invalid ones using approximate constraints. Evaluations demonstrate that lang latency and inherent difficulty of coordinating under partial observability and agent heterogeneity cause LLMs to frequently fail under dynamic environments and fall significantly short of a global-knowledge oracle.
deep_dive.llm_tldr_en: Presents a Minecraft-based benchmark for time-sensitive complementary collaboration tasks, addressing heterogeneity, mandatory collaboration, dynamic environments, and real-time constraints.
deep_dive.llm_tldr_cn: 该论文提出了一个基于Minecraft的时间敏感互补协作基准，涵盖了智能体异质性、强制协作、动态环境和严格实时约束等核心特性，并开发了TickingCollab框架以支持动态环境生成和声明式任务规范，为多智能体协作研究提供了可扩展的评估平台。
deep_dive.llm_tldr: 该论文提出了一个基于Minecraft的时间敏感互补协作基准，涵盖了智能体异质性、强制协作、动态环境和严格实时约束等核心特性，并开发了TickingCollab框架以支持动态环境生成和声明式任务规范，为多智能体协作研究提供了可扩展的评估平台。

quick_skim.title: Exit-and-Join Dynamics for Decentralized Coalition Formation
quick_skim.abstract: This paper studies coalition formation as a decentralized dynamical process driven by unilateral exit-and-join decisions. Agents evaluate local moves using the Aumann-Dreze value, so payoffs are computed within the agent's current coalition rather than through a globally negotiated coalition structure. The resulting model links cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-response behavior: a terminal partition is precisely a coalition structure with no admissible, individually profitable exit-and-join deviation. We establish equilibrium characterizations, identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a special convex-game benchmark.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: decentralized coalition formation with cooperative payoff allocation
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 分散式联盟形成中的合作收益分配
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 分散式联盟形成中的合作收益分配
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 分散式联盟形成中的合作收益分配
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: Proposes a decentralized dynamical process for coalition formation where agents make unilateral exit-and-join decisions based on Aumann-Dreze value, linking cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best response.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: 本文研究联盟形成作为由单边退出与加入决策驱动的分散动力学过程。代理使用Aumann-Dreze值评估局部移动，将合作收益分配与非合作最佳响应行为联系起来。建立了均衡特征刻画，识别了标量Lyapunov势能条件，并分析了切换成本对局部稳定性的影响。为多智能体系统中合作联盟的自组织提供了新的博弈论视角。
quick_skim.llm_tldr: 本文研究联盟形成作为由单边退出与加入决策驱动的分散动力学过程。代理使用Aumann-Dreze值评估局部移动，将合作收益分配与非合作最佳响应行为联系起来。建立了均衡特征刻画，识别了标量Lyapunov势能条件，并分析了切换成本对局部稳定性的影响。为多智能体系统中合作联盟的自组织提供了新的博弈论视角。

quick_skim.title: Multi-Agent Transactive Memory
quick_skim.abstract: The decentralized deployment of LLM agents with diverse capabilities across diverse tasks motivates infrastructure for knowledge sharing across heterogeneous agent populations. Just as search engines index human-generated artifacts to support human problem solving, retrieval systems can organize agent-generated artifacts for reuse across agent populations. We extend retrieval-augmented generation - which demonstrates the value of human-authored artifacts to individual agents - to retrieval of agent-generated artifacts supporting a population of agents. In particular, agent trajectories encode reusable procedural knowledge, yet these artifacts are typically discarded after a single use or retained only by the producing agent, forcing newly instantiated agents to repeatedly rediscover existing solutions. We propose Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM), a framework for population-level storage and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories, where producer agents contribute trajectories to a shared repository and consumer agents retrieve them to improve task execution. We focus on interactive environments (ALFWorld and WebArena), where trajectories are long and encode especially rich procedural structure. Our experiments demonstrate that retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps without coordination or joint training. These results position MATM as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.
quick_skim.llm_evidence_en: multi-agent knowledge sharing in decentralized LLM agents
quick_skim.llm_evidence_cn: 去中心化LLM代理间的多智能体知识共享
quick_skim.llm_evidence: 去中心化LLM代理间的多智能体知识共享
quick_skim.canonical_evidence: 去中心化LLM代理间的多智能体知识共享
quick_skim.llm_tldr_en: Decentralized LLM agents share procedural knowledge via retrieval-augmented generation to avoid redundant exploration.
quick_skim.llm_tldr_cn: 针对分散部署的LLM代理重复发现已有解决方案的问题，本文提出多智能体交易记忆，通过检索增强生成在代理间共享轨迹信息，实验表明显著降低新代理的探索成本，提升了异构代理群体的效率。
quick_skim.llm_tldr: 针对分散部署的LLM代理重复发现已有解决方案的问题，本文提出多智能体交易记忆，通过检索增强生成在代理间共享轨迹信息，实验表明显著降低新代理的探索成本，提升了异构代理群体的效率。
quick_skim.matched_query_text: Papers central to 多智能体强化学习, especially work that connects or combines: multi-agent reinforcement learning; cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning; decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning; centralized training for multi-agent reinforcement learning; decentralized execution in multi-agent systems; Markov games in multi-agent reinforcement learning; multi agent reinforcement learning algorithms; cooperative multi agent reinforcement learning applications; decentralized multi agent reinforcement learning methods; comparison of value decomposition and actor critic methods.
